summary: Gruber examines the European Data Protection Board's draft ruling against Meta's 'pay or consent to tracking' model, which would require large platforms to offer a free tier without behavioral advertising. He argues this is a radical and selectively enforced stance, noting that EU-based companies like Der Spiegel and Spotify use identical or similar models without facing scrutiny. He outlines Meta's limited compliance options, each of which carries significant business risk, and suggests that if Meta caves to reduced ARPU in the EU, regulators worldwide will demand the same concessions. Gruber concludes that EU regulators may be surprised by how Meta responds.
key_points: The EDPB ruling effectively mandates that large platforms offer a free tier without behavioral advertising, going beyond the existing 'pay or consent' model
EU-based companies like Der Spiegel and Spotify use the same pay-or-consent model but aren't targeted because they weren't designated as 'gatekeepers'
Meta's compliance options all carry severe business risks, from revenue-destroying free tiers to the nuclear option of withdrawing from the EU entirely
If Meta complies by accepting lower ARPU in the EU, it creates a template for regulators worldwide to demand the same, threatening Meta's entire business model
The ruling selectively targets large non-EU companies while giving domestic companies a pass on identical practices
money_quotes: They're not saying 'pay or OK' is illegal. They're saying it's illegal only if you're a big company from outside the EU with a very popular platform.
This is the 'nerd harder' fantasy solution, a la demanding that secure end-to-end encryption provide back doors available only to 'the good guys'.

But if Meta caves and complies with this ruling by offering a free tier with significantly lower ARPU, that opens the door for regulators and legislative bodies around the globe to demand the same. Then, poof goes Meta as an industry colossus.
I suspect the EU regulatory bodies have some surprises coming regarding how this is going to play out.
Christ, they don't even let you look at their homepage without paying or consenting to targeted ads.
But Spotify is an EU company, so of course it wasn't designated as a 'gatekeeper' by the protection racketeers running the European Commission.
key_insight: The EU's selective enforcement of 'pay or consent' rules against large non-EU platforms while ignoring identical practices by domestic companies reveals the regulatory regime as protectionism masquerading as privacy advocacy.
